title: Rotate the array

content.problemStatement:
You are given an integer array numsnumsnums. Rotate the array to the right by kkk positions, where kkk is a non-negative integer. 

**For example**: 

Given the array [10, 20, 30, 40, 50], rotating it two times to the right results in: 

First rotation - [50, 10, 20, 30, 40] 

Second rotation - [40, 50, 10, 20, 30] 

You need to implement a solution that operates **in-place** and uses only **O(1)O(1)O(1)** additional space.

You don’t need to print any values; simply edit the values of the array.

Function DeclarationFunction Namerotaterotaterotate – This function rotates an array to the right by kkk positions in-place.

Parameters
numsnumsnums : An array of integers.
kkk : A non-negative integer representing the number of right rotations.
Return Value
This function does **not** return anything.
The array numsnumsnums is modified in-place.
Constraints
1≤nums.length≤1051 \leq nums.length \leq 10^51≤nums.length≤105
−231≤nums[i]≤231−1−2^\text{31} \leq nums[i] \leq 2^\text{31} − 1−231≤nums[i]≤231−1
0≤k≤1090 \leq k \leq 10^90≤k≤109
Rotation must be done using **O(1)** extra space
Follow up:
Can you think of multiple approaches to solve this problem? (There are at least **three** different strategies.)

Input Format

The first line contains two integers NNN and KKK — the size of the array and the number of right rotations.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers representing the array elements.


Output Format

Print the rotated array in a line (space-separated).
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains two integers N and K — the size of the array and the number of right rotations. The second line contains N space‑separated integers representing the array elements.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing the array after rotating it to the right by K positions. Elements must be separated by a single space.

sampleTestCases.explanation: K=2, N=5. Rotating right by 2 moves the last two elements (40,50) to the front.
K=3, N=5. The last three elements (9,1,2) become the first three.
K=2, N=4. After rotation the array is [3,4,1,2].

solution.approach: The key observation is that a right rotation by K positions can be achieved by three reversals. First reverse the whole array, then reverse the first K elements, and finally reverse the remaining N‑K elements. After these steps the array is exactly the original array shifted right by K. This works because reversal changes the order of elements, and the three‑step process restores the relative order of the two parts while moving the suffix to the front. The algorithm runs in O(N) time per test case and uses only O(1) extra space (a few integer variables).
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the size of the array. The total complexity is O( Σ N ) over all test cases.

code.Java: import java.io.*;import java.util.*;public class Main{private static void reverse(int[] a,int l,int r){while(l<r){int tmp=a[l];a[l]=a[r];a[r]=tmp;l++;r--;}}private static void rotate(int[] a,long k){int n=a.length;if(n==0)return;k%=n;if(k==0)return;reverse(a,0,n-1);reverse(a,0,(int)k-1);reverse(a,(int)k,n-1);}public static void main(String[] args)throws Exception{BufferedReader br=new BufferedReader(new InputStreamReader(System.in));StringBuilder out=new StringBuilder();int T=Integer.parseInt(br.readLine().trim());while(T-->0){StringTokenizer st=new StringTokenizer(br.readLine());int N=Integer.parseInt(st.nextToken());long K=Long.parseLong(st.nextToken());int[] arr=new int[N];st=new StringTokenizer(br.readLine());for(int i=0;i<N;i++)arr[i]=Integer.parseInt(st.nextToken());rotate(arr,K);for(int i=0;i<N;i++){if(i>0)out.append(' ');out.append(arr[i]);}out.append('\n');}System.out.print(out.toString());}}
